title: Quality Loss from Distillation
concept: Aggressively distilled models sample much faster but often sacrifice some image quality or diversity compared to the full many-step process.

exercises.prompt: If you cook a soup in 5 minutes instead of 5 hours, what might you have to give up?

options.text: Some of the deep, rich flavor
The pot you cooked it in

exercises.explain: Speeding things up often means trading away a little quality for a lot of saved time!

cards.title: Faster Isn't Always Free
cards.body: We learned that distillation lets AI models create images super fast, sometimes in just one step! But speed can come with a hidden price tag. What might we be trading away?

cards.title: The Quality Trade-off
cards.body: Aggressively distilled models (ones squeezed down to very few steps) usually sample much faster, but they can lose some image quality or variety compared to the slow, full many-step process.

cards.title: Instant Noodles vs Slow-Cooked Ramen
cards.body: Instant noodles are ready in 3 minutes and taste fine. Slow-cooked ramen takes hours but has richer flavor and more depth. Distilled AI models are like instant noodles: quick and handy, but not always as rich as the original slow version.

cards.title: One-Step vs Fifty-Step
cards.body: A model that draws a cat in 1 step might make a decent cat picture fast. The same model using 50 steps might make a more detailed, more varied cat picture — but it takes much longer to create.

cards.title: Myth: Faster Always Means Better
cards.body: Myth: If a model got faster, it must have gotten better too. Truth: Speed and quality are often a trade-off. Aggressive distillation can boost speed a lot while quietly lowering detail or variety.

cards.title: Speed vs Detail
cards.body: Compare a heavily distilled fast model to the original many-step model.

left.text: Very fast, but images can look slightly less detailed or less varied

right.text: Slow, but produces richer detail and more diverse results

exercises.prompt: What is a common downside of aggressively distilled models?

options.text: They can lose some image quality or diversity
They become impossible to run on computers

exercises.explain: Distillation speeds things up but can sacrifice some richness and variety in the results.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Distilled models are always exactly as good as the original many-step model, just faster.
exercises.explain: False! Aggressive distillation often trades away some quality or diversity in exchange for speed.

exercises.sentence: Aggressively distilled models sample much faster but may sacrifice some image ___ or diversity.
exercises.explain: The main trade-off is losing some quality or diversity in exchange for speed.

exercises.prompt: Why might an artist choose the slower, many-step model instead of the fast distilled one?

options.text: Because they want the richest, most detailed and varied result, even if it takes longer
Because slow models are always cheaper to run
Because fast models can't create any images at all

exercises.explain: When quality and variety matter more than speed, the full many-step process is often preferred.

exercises.prompt: A game studio needs to generate thousands of quick concept art sketches for brainstorming, then later wants one polished final piece. Which approach makes the most sense?

options.text: Use a fast distilled model for the many quick sketches, then the full many-step model for the final polished piece
Use the full many-step model for every single quick sketch
Use the distilled model for the final polished piece only
Never use distilled models for anything

exercises.explain: This balances the trade-off wisely: speed for quick brainstorming, and quality for the final result.
